coredata.dc:title: HMTA-assisted uniform cobalt ions activated copper oxide microspheres with enhanced electrochemical performance for pseudocapacitors
coredata.dc:creator: Hussain, Sk. Khaja
Yu, Jae Su
coredata.dc:description: Supercapacitors are being considered to be one of important electrochemical energy storage devices due to their high power density as compared with batteries and have remarkable applications. The large surface area transition metal oxides or hydroxides are excellent conductive materials and have been extensively used for supercapacitors. Herein, uniform copper oxide (CuO) and cobalt (Co) activated CuO (CuO:Co) microspheres were synthesized by a hexamethylenetetramine (HMTA)-assisted hydrothermal method. The HMTA was used as an additive surfactant to acquire the uniform microsphere morphology, which was confirmed by high-resolution field-emission scanning electron microscope and field-emission transmission electron microscope images. The phase form of the pristine CuO and CuO:Co powder samples was examined by X-ray diffraction patterns. For the application of pseudocapacitors, electrochemical studies were performed for the CuO and CuO:Co electrode materials in a three-electrode electrochemical cell system. The cyclic voltammetry and galvanostatic charge-discharge curves were measured at different scan rates and current densities, respectively, in 1 M KOH electrolyte solution. The calculated specific capacitance values of the CuO and CuO:Co electrodes were 77 and 284 F/g at a current density of 1 A/g, respectively. Interestingly, by incorporating small concentration of Co into CuO, the specific capacitance was enhanced approximately 4 times when compared with the pristine CuO electrode. The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy spectrum was analyzed for the pseudocapacitive behavior of CuO:Co electrode material. Furthermore, the CuO:Co electrode showed a capacitance retention (75.6%) after 1000 cycles at a current density of 3 A/g. Therefore, the obtained remarkable electrochemical results suggest that the low-cost CuO:Co electrode materials with improved electrochemical performance have promising applications in the field of pseudocapacitors.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Cobalt activated copper oxide
Uniform microspheres
Hexamethylenetetramine
Hydrothermal method
Pseudocapacitors

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Ministry of Science, ICT and Future Planning

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Research Foundation of Korea grant funded by the Korea government (MSIP) (No. 2017R1A2B4011998 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) FE-SEM image of the prepared CuO, and (b) low-magnification and (c) high-magnification FE-SEM images of the CuO:Co microspheres. Inset shows the magnified FE-SEM image of the CuO:Co single microsphere. (d) FE-TEM image, (e) high-magnification FE-TEM image, (f) HRTEM image and (g) SAED pattern of the single CuO:Co microsphere. (h) EDX spectrum and (i–k) elemental mappings of the CuO:Co microsphere.
(a) XRD patterns and (b) FTIR spectra of the CuO and CuO:Co microspheres.
(a) XPS survey scan spectrum of the CuO:Co microspheres and high-magnification survey scan spectra of (b) Cu 2p, (c) Co 2p and (d) O 1s.

Comparative (a) CV curves and (b) GCD curves of the CuO and CuO:Co electrode materials at a constant scan rate of 30 mV/s and current density of 1 A/g. (c) CV curves at various scan rates and (d) GCD curves as a function of current density for the CuO:Co electrode material in 1 M KOH electrolyte solution.

(a) Calculated specific capacitance values of the CuO and CuO:Co electrodes at different current densities. (b) EIS spectrum and (c) cycling stability test of the CuO:Co electrode at a current density of 3 A/g in 1 M KOH electrolyte solution.
Schematic diagram for the synthesis of uniform CuO:Co microspheres.

head.ce:title: HMTA-assisted uniform cobalt ions activated copper oxide microspheres with enhanced electrochemical performance for pseudocapacitors

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Electronic Engineering, Institute for Wearable Convergence Electronics, Kyung Hee University, Yongin-si, Gyeonggi-do 17104, Republic of Korea

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Supercapacitors are being considered to be one of important electrochemical energy storage devices due to their high power density as compared with batteries and have remarkable applications. The large surface area transition metal oxides or hydroxides are excellent conductive materials and have been extensively used for supercapacitors. Herein, uniform copper oxide (CuO) and cobalt (Co) activated CuO (CuO:Co) microspheres were synthesized by a hexamethylenetetramine (HMTA)-assisted hydrothermal method. The HMTA was used as an additive surfactant to acquire the uniform microsphere morphology, which was confirmed by high-resolution field-emission scanning electron microscope and field-emission transmission electron microscope images. The phase form of the pristine CuO and CuO:Co powder samples was examined by X-ray diffraction patterns. For the application of pseudocapacitors, electrochemical studies were performed for the CuO and CuO:Co electrode materials in a three-electrode electrochemical cell system. The cyclic voltammetry and galvanostatic charge-discharge curves were measured at different scan rates and current densities, respectively, in 1 M KOH electrolyte solution. The calculated specific capacitance values of the CuO and CuO:Co electrodes were 77 and 284 F/g at a current density of 1 A/g, respectively. Interestingly, by incorporating small concentration of Co into CuO, the specific capacitance was enhanced approximately 4 times when compared with the pristine CuO electrode. The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy spectrum was analyzed for the pseudocapacitive behavior of CuO:Co electrode material. Furthermore, the CuO:Co electrode showed a capacitance retention (75.6%) after 1000 cycles at a current density of 3 A/g. Therefore, the obtained remarkable electrochemical results suggest that the low-cost CuO:Co electrode materials with improved electrochemical performance have promising applications in the field of pseudocapacitors.

ce:section.ce:para: Recently, supercapacitors are rapidly gaining considerable attention and have wide applications in energy storage devices because of their intrinsic long time cycling stability, prominent power density compared to batteries and higher energy density than traditional capacitors [1–4]. Based on the electrochemical performance and energy storage process, these superior features of supercapacitors are mainly categorized into electric double-layer capacitors (EDLCs) and pseudocapacitors [5–7]. In case of the EDLCs, their capacitance is induced from the electrostatic charges and they store the energy at the interface between the electrode and electrolyte. Graphene, graphene oxide and carbon nanotube consisting of Sp2 hybridized carbon atoms have received much attention due to their excellent mechanical strength, low density and high electrical conductivity [8–10]. Because of these superior characteristics, the carbon-based materials have been extensively applied in various research fields such as batteries, fuel cells, chemical sensors, biosensors, nanoelectronic devices and electrocatalytic applications [11–14]. Herein, in EDLCs, the electrode materials fabricated from the activated carbon, reduced graphene and carbon nanotubes with larger surface area and more porosity have been widely used as active materials [5,15,16]. On the other hand, pseudocapacitors are built based on the reversible faradic reduction and oxidation (redox) electrochemical reactions, and they store the electric charge in between the electrode materials and electrolytes [17–19]. In pseudocapacitors, the generation of faradic current during charge-discharge process gives a relatively high specific capacitance compared to EDLCs. To date, many reports have been made on the development of pseudocapacitors with theoretical supports and practical evidences [4,5,16]. However, still some challenges remain in order to improve the poor cycling stability and low energy density in the electrode material for pseudocapacitors. Recently, mixed transition metal oxides or incorporation of metal ions into the host layer transition metal oxides have been attracting great interest owing to their multiple oxidation states as well as high specific capacitance and energy density values [20,21]. The mixed transition metal oxide electrode materials possess good ion transport, high structural stability and rapid electrode kinetics, and thus enhance the electroactive interface between the electrode and electrolyte. These superior electrochemical characteristics of the electrode materials exhibited better cycling stability and capacity when compared with the mono-metallic oxide electrode materials [22,23]. Consequently, it is rational to design novel mixed or metal ions incorporated transition metal oxide electrode materials with high energy density and excellent electrochemical properties for energy storage device applications.

A class of oxide and hydroxyl form of transition metals (Mn, Co, Ni and Cu) has received intense attention for ideal supercapacitors due to their variety of oxidation states which endow the effective redox charge transfer mechanism [24,25]. It is well known that ruthenium oxide is a traditional metal oxide material, showing higher specific capacitance, but high cost and toxicity limits its practical applications [25]. Therefore, the present research is focusing on eco-friendly and low-cost transition metal oxide materials to open up the application of supercapacitors. Among the available transition metal oxides, copper oxide (CuO) has received considerable interest and was used for various applications such as catalysis, solar cells, magnetic phase transitions, gas sensors, photodetectors and superconductors because of its cost-effectiveness, large abundance, chemical stability and eco-friendly nature [26–28]. It was reported that CuO was used as a negative electrode material in the field of lithium ion batteries [29]. The CuO synthesized by different experimental techniques with different morphologies like nanoflowers, nanorods, nanosheets, nanoribbons and micro-flowers has unique optical characteristics and also exhibits reliable electrochemical performance for supercapacitors [30]. The preparation of CuO electrode with definite morphology provides the multiple large surface area and active sites to allow the rapid charge transport process between the electrode and electrolyte for good energy storage performance. Thus, the fabrication of the CuO electrodes with uniform morphology is attracting significant research interest to obtain better electrochemical properties. In order to enhance the electrochemical properties of the electrodes with excellent capacitance values, it would be of great interest to design a hybrid electrode material by combing/doping the small amount of the other transition metals into the CuO during the fabrication process [31,32]. It is well-known that cobalt (Co) has been considered as a promising transition metal and exhibited superior electrochemical properties in the field of psudeocapcitors. Therefore, it is worth to investigate the incorporation of Co ions into CuO with well-defined morphology, which is a promising approach to exhibit excellent capacitive properties.

In this work, we prepared the novel CuO and Co activated CuO (CuO:Co) microspheres with good uniformity by a single-step hydrothermal method using hexamethylenetetramine as a surfactant. The structural and morphological properties of the powder samples were investigated in detail. The synthesized CuO and CuO:Co powder form of the samples was further designed as electrode materials for pseudocapacitors in alkaline 1 M KOH electrolyte solution. Remarkably, the Co activated CuO electrode showed higher specific capacitance than the CuO electrode with good cycling stability.

Copper(II) acetate monohydrate ((CO2CH3)2Cu·H2O), cobalt(II) acetate tetrahydrate ((CH3COO)2Co·4H2O) and hexamethylenetetramine (HMTA, C6H12N4) were purchased from Sigma Aldrich Co., South Korea. N-methyl-2-pyrrolidone (NMP, C5H9NO) and Polyvinylidene fluoride (PVdF, –(C2H2F2)n–) were delivered from Daejung Chemicals Ltd, South Korea. All the chemicals were of analytical grade purity and used as received without any further purification. De-ionized (DI) water was utilized during the synthesis process and it was made from a Milli-Q water purifier system having a resistivity of 18.2 MΩ cm.

CuO uniform microspheres were synthesized by a hydrothermal technique. Typically, 20 mmol of (CO2CH3)2Cu·H2O was dissolved in 80 mL of DI water as the Cu(II) source under stirring at 500 rpm. After 15 min, the double amount (40 mmol) of HMTA was added to the Cu(II) salt solution and the stirring was maintained for 30 min to get a complete homogeneous solution. The homogenously mixed solution was transferred into a Teflon-lined autoclave (100 mL volume) and it was fixed tightly. The autoclave was then heated to 140 °C at a rate of 2 °C/min and preserved the temperature for 2 h without any magnetic stirring. After the progressive reaction time, the autoclave temperature was slowly decreased to room temperature and then the black color precipitate was collected by centrifugation at 3000 rpm for 5 min. The collected precipitate was washed with DI water and ethanol 3 times and centrifuged. The centrifuged product was kept for drying at 120 °C for 12 h in oven. Similarly, the experiment was repeated by introducing the x = 3 mmol amount of (CH3COO)2Co·4H2O as the Co(II) source into the (20-x) mmol (CO2CH3)2Cu·H2O and HMTA solution. Finally, the physicochemical properties of the resulting CuO and Co activated CuO samples were characterized. A simple schematic diagram for the synthesis of uniform CuO:Co microspheres can be seen in Scheme 1 .

A high-resolution field-emission scanning electron microscope (HR FE-SEM, CARL ZEISS MERLIN) which is attached with the energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) system and field-emission transmission electron microscope (FE-TEM, JEOL JEM 2100F) equipment were used to investigate the morphologies of the synthesized CuO and Co activated CuO (CuO:Co) samples. The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra of the CuO:Co sample were recorded on a FTIR spectrophotometer with KBr pellet technique. The phases and electronic states of the elements in the synthesized CuO and CuO:Co samples were analyzed by using an X-ray powder diffractometer (Mac Science M18XHF-SRA) and an X-ray photoelectric spectrometer (Thermo Multi-Lab 2000 system), respectively.

For the preparation of working electrode, initially a piece of Ni foam sheet was dipped into a 1 M HCl solution and sonicated to ensure the removal of oxide layer on the Ni foam. After that, it was washed with DI water several times, dried with a stream of N2 gas and cut into 1 × 1 cm2. The working electrode was prepared by the homogeneous mixing of 80 wt% uniform CuO and CuO:Co microspheres with the 10 wt% conductive carbon black (super P) and 10 wt% commercially available PVdF binder. By using the agate mortar, the above mixture was ground well for about 20–30 min and few drops of NMP solvent were added to form a slurry. Later, the prepared slurry was uniformly coated on the well-cleaned Ni foam and kept drying at room temperature for 30 min. Finally, the prepared electrodes were kept on a hot plate at 140 °C for 3 h. The synthesized uniform CuO and CuO:Co microspheres coated on Ni foam were referred as the working electrode. At room temperature, the electrochemical performance of the samples was tested in a beaker-type three-electrode electrochemical cell system with the working electrode, the Ag/AgCl reference/standard electrode and the Pt wire as a counter electrode. Freshly prepared 1 M aqueous KOH solution (pH ∼13.9) was used as the electrolyte solution for the measurement of three-electrode electrochemical cell system. In general, the determination of capacitance values mainly depends on the electrochemical cell configuration system and the mass of the active materials on the electrode. The three-electrode system provides relatively high testing values and the data obtained in this system are more consistent as compared with the two-electrode system. The determination of capacitance by two-electrode system depends on the equilibrium potentials of positive and negative electrodes. By increasing the operating voltage of the capacitor, the potential of the positive electrode is shifted in the positive direction, which results in lower capacitance values. Thus, three-electrode system is more rational to check the electrochemical performance of the prepared electrode materials. Accordingly, the cyclic voltammetry (CV), galvanostatic charge-discharge (GCD) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements were performed in the three-electrode electrochemical cell system with an IviumStat electrochemical workstation (Ivium Technologies; Netherlands).

The morphological properties of the as-synthesized CuO and CuO:Co samples were examined by FE-SEM images, as depicted in Fig. 1 . After hydrothermal treatment, the typical FE-SEM image of the CuO sample exhibited the uniform microspheres with an average size of 1 μm as shown in Fig. 1(a). The uniform microspherical morphology was retained for the Co activated CuO sample, and it can be observed in the low-magnification (Fig. 1(b)) and high-magnification (Fig. 1(c)) FE-SEM images. For more clarity, the high-magnification FE-SEM image of a single CuO:Co microsphere is shown in the inset of Fig. 1(c). With the help of HMTA surfactant, the possible formation of uniform CuO microspheres can be predicted as follows. As is well known, HMTA is a weak base and plays a significant role in forming a definite morphology and it is also a source for the generation of the hydroxyl (OH−) ions at elevated temperatures [33–35]. Firstly, the following preliminary reactions occurred during the synthesis process when (CO2CH3)2Cu·H2O and HMTA were added to the DI water:
(1) Cu(CH3COO)2 + 2H2O → Cu(OH)2 + 2CH3COOH
(2) (CH2)6N4 + 6H2O → 6HCHO + 4NH3
(3)
4 NH
3
+
H - OH ↔
NH
4
OH ↔
4 NH
4 +
+
OH
−
From the above equations, it is clear that, when HMTA reacts with water molecules at supplied temperature, it gives formaldehyde and ammonia due to the decomposition process. The liberated ammonia further underwent hydrolysis and produced OH− ions. Consequently, the released OH− ions from HMTA in the solution led to the formation of the Cu(OH)2 nuclei. The concentrations of Cu2+ ions and OH− ions were exceeded at desired experimental conditions and the Cu(OH)2 nuclei can be transferred into the uniform CuO microspheres:
(4)
Cu
( OH )
2
→ Δ
C u O +
H 2
O
In order to evaluate the detailed morphological features and particle size of the synthesized Co activated CuO samples, FE-TEM analysis was carried out. Fig. 1(d) shows the FE-TEM image of the CuO:Co single microsphere having the particle size ∼1 μm and it is in good accordance with the FE-SEM results. Furthermore, the edge part of the single microsphere (Fig. 1(e)) showed a porous nature, which is beneficial for efficient electrolyte penetration during the electrochemical measurements. The corresponding high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image and selected area diffractions (SAED) further provide the supreme structural properties of the synthesized microspheres as shown in Fig. 1(f) and 1(g). From the SAED patterns, it can be noticeable that a number of bright spots were in circular manner with the corresponding (0 2 1) and (0 0 2) reflections, confirming the polycrystalline nature. The EDX spectrum (Fig. 1(h)) and FE-SEM elemental color mapping (Fig. 1(i)-1(k)) results were measured for the uniform CuO:Co microspheres. The EDX spectrum displayed the existence of the Cu, O and Co elements with their corresponding weight percentage values and no other elements except the Cu, O and Co were observed.

X-ray photoelectric spectroscopy (XPS) analysis is one of the important techniques to determine the oxidation state and chemical composition of the synthesized uniform Co doped CuO microspheres. Fig. 3 shows the XPS survey scan spectra of the CuO:Co sample consisting of Cu 2p, Co 2p and O 1s peaks, indicating the existence of Cu, Co and O elements, respectively, in the synthesized uniform CuO:Co microspheres. From the XPS spectrum (Fig. 3(a)), the peak at approximately 285 eV is related to the C 1s and further it can be utilized as a reference to record the binding energies of the CuO:Co sample. As is shown in Fig. 3(b), two major peaks at 933.8 and 958.3 eV along with their adjacent shake-up satellite peaks belong to the corresponding Cu 2p3/2 and Cu 2p1/2 core levels, respectively [38]. Thus, the occurrence of the main peaks at the Cu 2p3/2 and Cu 2p1/2 levels in the high-resolution XPS spectrum indicates the spin-orbit characteristic of Cu2+ ions in CuO:Co sample. In the similar way, the Cu3d9 shell was not identified in the spectrum, which further supports to the presence of the Cu2+ oxidation state in the prepared sample [26]. Additionally, the high-resolution XPS spectrum of Co 2p is depicted in Fig. 3(c). The binding energy values around at 780.5 and 796 eV were noticed and they are related to Co 2p3/2 and Co 2p1/2 peaks, which indicates the existence of cobalt in 2 + oxidation state for the synthesized CuO:Co sample [39,40]. On the other hand, the O 1s core level spectrum (Fig. 3(d)) revealed broad peaks having the binding energy values at 525.5 and 531.2 eV, which are analogous to the existence of oxygen in the Co doped CuO uniform microspheres.

The electrochemical performance of the prepared CuO and CuO:Co samples was examined in a three-electrode cell configuration system using 1 M KOH electrolyte solution. Fig. 4 (a) shows the comparative CV curves of the uniform CuO and Co activated CuO microsphere electrode materials (which are coated on the Ni foam) at a constant scan rate of 30 mV/s in the potential range of 0.0–0.6 V (vs. Ag/AgCl). From the CV curve of the CuO microspheres, a pair of redox peaks were observed. This is because of the revisable reactions in between the monovalent and divalent copper ion spices in the alkaline electrolyte solution, indicating that the prepared electrode material shows the pseudocapacitive behavior. However, the CV curve of the Co activated CuO microspheres showed higher integrated area along with the couple of strong faradic redox peaks. The electrochemical capacitances of the uniform CuO and CuO:Co microspheres mainly benefit from the Cu+/Cu2+ and Co2+/Co3+ faradic redox pairs and the OH− ions in the KOH electrolyte solution. Consequently, the following electrochemical reactions occurred due to the faradic redox reactions of the Cu and Co species in the KOH electrolyte solution [41–43].
(5) 1/2Cu2O + OH− ↔ CuO + 1/2H2O + e−
(6) 1/2Cu2O + 1/2H2O + OH− ↔ Cu(OH)2 + e−
(7) Cu(OH) + OH− ↔ CuO + H2O + e−
(8) Cu(OH) + OH− ↔ Cu(OH)2 + e−
(9) CoO + OH− ↔ CoOOH + e−
(10) CoOOH + OH− ↔ CoO2 + H2O + e−
To calculate the specific capacitances of the prepared CuO and CuO:Co electrodes, the GCD measurements were performed in the potential window of 0.0–0.5 V. Fig. 4(b) shows the comparative GCD curves of the CuO and CuO:Co electrode materials at a constant current density of 1 A/g. From the GCD curves, the nonlinear charge-discharge profiles in both samples indicate the pseudocapacitive behavior via the faradic redox reactions of the Cu+/Cu2+ and Co2+/Co3+ ions in the alkaline electrolyte solution, which is consistent with the CV curves. Moreover, it is clear that the time taken for the discharge process for the Co activated CuO electrode is longer when compared with the CuO electrode material, which authenticates the higher specific capacitance for the CuO:Co electrode. The higher charge-discharge time value for the CuO:Co electrode can be attributed to the substitution of highly conductive and electrochemically active Co species. In addition, the porous features of CuO:Co provide the rapid paths for diffusion of electrolyte ions to favor the reversible faradic redox reactions. To further study the specific capacitance and rate capability of the optimized CuO:Co material, CV and GCD analyses were carried out. Fig. 4(c) shows the CV curves of the as-prepared CuO:Co electrode at different scan rates (5–100 mV/s) in 1 M KOH electrolyte solution. For comparison, the CV curves of pristine CuO under different scan rates are also presented in Fig. S1(a) (Supporting Information). The CV curves of the both CuO and Co activated CuO electrode materials at higher scan rates exhibited a similar pseudocapacitive behavior except the variation in the current peak values, which indicates that the electrodes possess good electrochemical reversibility. In order to obtain the better capacitance performance and rate capability values for the prepared CuO and CuO:Co electrode materials, GCD measurements were carried out in 1 M KOH electrolyte solution by varying the current density from 1 to 11 A/g in the 0.0–0.5 V potential range as shown in Fig. S1(b) and Fig. 4(d), respectively. At different current densities, the GCD curves of the both electrodes exhibited asymmetrical properties, which provides the further confirmation to the pseudocapacitive behavior of the prepared electrode materials.

The specific capacitance values can be determined for the both electrode materials based on the following equation [44–46]:
(11)
C
sc
=
I Δ t
m Δ V
.
Here, Csc, Δt, I, m and ΔV represent the specific capacitance (F/g), discharge time (s), discharge current (A), mass (g) and potential window (V) of the designed CuO and CuO:Co electrode materials, respectively. The calculated specific capacitance values for the CuO and CuO:Co electrodes as a function of current density were compared, as depicted in Fig. 5 (a). From the obtained results, the Co activated CuO electrode showed higher specific capacitance value of 284 F/g at 1 A/g of current density which was approximately 4 times greater when compared to the prepared CuO electrode (77 F/g). The obtained high specific capacitance values of Co activated CuO were also higher than of those of the earlier reports based on CuO materials, as can be seen in Table S1. The reason for the enhancement of capacitance value in the CuO:Co electrode is the quick faradic redox reaction of the CuO and Co in the CuO:Co composite, which shortens the electronic paths and increases the kinetics of the electrode materials [47,48]. Thus, the acceleration of the electron transfer in the CuO:Co electrode was enhanced to store the improved energy. Under the maximum current density of 11 A/g, the specific capacitance (128 F/g) of Co activated CuO electrode exhibited a good rate capability value of 45% when compared with the initial specific capacitance at 1 A/g of current density. The EIS curve is one of the characteristic techniques to check the electrochemical behavior of the prepared CuO:Co electrode and it was analyzed by Nyquist plot (Fig. 5(b)). The typical Nyquist plot was measured in the frequency range from 0.01 Hz to 100 kHz with an amplitude of 5 mV in 1 M KOH electrolyte solution. From the EIS spectrum, a semicircle in the higher frequency regions and a sloped line with ∼45° portion of angle in the lower frequency regions can be observed. The intersection of the semicircle in the higher frequency regions represents the equivalent series internal resistance (R
s ) at the electrode/electrolyte interface, which governs the power capability and the corresponding diameter indicates the existence of the charge-transfer resistance (R
ct ). The sloped line from the EIS spectrum is the Warburg resistance which is associated with the diffusion/transport of the ions in the CuO:Co electrode material. From the Nyquist plot, the R
ct value was found to be 2.69 Ω. Thus, the obtained low R
ct value further implies that the prepared CuO:Co electrode material exhibited good electrochemical conductivity. In general, cyclic stability is a main criterion to justify the performance of the prepared electrode materials for the application of supercapacitors. Fig. 5(c) shows the calculated long-term cycle stability of the CuO:Co electrode material by continuous GCD measurements up to 1000 cycles at 3 A/g of current density in the 0.0–0.5 V potential range. In Fig. 5(c), it can be seen that at 1000 cycles, the specific capacitance was 158 F/g with a loss of 24.4% with respect to the initial cycle capacitance (209 F/g). The loss of specific capacitance (24.4%) not only depends on the characteristic property of the CuO:Co electrode material but also on the continuous redox reactions during the charge/discharge measurements up to 1000 cycles. The rapid redox reactions degraded the binding nature of the electrode with its PVdF matrix, which results in the change in the volume of the active materials [49]. Thus, CuO:Co electrode material exhibited the reduction in its specific capacitance value. Therefore, the CuO:Co electrode composite exhibited the capacitance retention of about 75.6% after 1000 cycles, which has great potential applications to develop the energy storage devices.

In summary, we have successfully synthesized uniform CuO and CuO:Co microspheres by a single-step hydrothermal method with the use of HMTA surfactant. The XRD, XPS and FTIR analyses confirmed the structure, existence of the oxidation states and functional groups for the synthesized CuO and CuO:Co samples. The FE-SEM images of the synthesized CuO and CuO:Co samples showed a uniform microsphere morphology and exhibited polycrystalline nature which was further confirmed by the FE-TEM images. The uniform CuO and CuO:Co microsphere electrode materials revealed good electrochemical properties in 1 M KOH electrolyte solution. The specific capacitance from the electrochemical analysis for the CuO electrode material at 1 A/g of current density was 77 F/g and it was further increased to 284 F/g by doping small concentration of Co into CuO under the same current density. The enhancement of specific capacitance value is owing to the quick faradic redox reaction of the CuO and Co in the CuO:Co electrode composite, which accelerates the electronic paths to store the improved energy performance. Furthermore, the CuO:Co electrode material exhibited a retention of specific capacitance (75.6%) after performing 1000 cycles at a high current density of 3 A/g. Consequently, the facile synthesis strategy, novel structural features and good electrochemical conductivity of the CuO:Co electrode material with high specific capacitance provide a deep insight into supercapacitors to be used as a positive electrode for energy storage device applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Research Foundation of Korea grant funded by the Korea government (MSIP) (No. 2017R1A2B4011998).

ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the supplementary data related to this article:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.electacta.2017.11.073.
